lessons.content: German present tense is called Präsens. It describes what is happening now, what you regularly do, or general facts. Unlike English, German has only one present tense form — it covers both English simple present and present continuous. Präsens appears in every section of the exam. In Schreiben, you describe yourself, your family, your habits. In Sprechen, you answer questions about your daily life. In Hören and Lesen, you follow conversations about everyday situations. Master Präsens and you cover at least 70% of the grammar you need for the entire exam.

exercises.question: German "Präsens" covers which English tenses?
exercises.hint: Think about "I learn" vs "I am learning" — how many German forms exist for both?
options: Neither — German uses a separate continuous tense
exercises.explanation: German Präsens covers BOTH English simple present ("I learn") and present continuous ("I am learning"). "Ich lerne Deutsch" means either "I learn German" (habit) or "I am learning German" (right now) — context tells you which. German does not have a separate continuous form. One tense does both jobs.
exercises.rule_hint: Ich lerne = I learn AND I am learning. One German Präsens covers both English present forms.

lessons.title: Regular verbs — one pattern, every verb
lessons.content: German has hundreds of regular verbs. They all follow the same pattern. Learn it once and you can conjugate any regular verb immediately. Step 1: find the stem by removing the -en from the infinitive. Step 2: add the correct ending for each pronoun.

exercises.question: Amara ___ (lernen) jeden Tag Deutsch. Choose the correct form.
exercises.hint: Amara is "she" — which ending does er/sie/es take with a regular verb?
exercises.explanation: "lernt" — Amara is she (sie), so we use the er/sie/es form. Regular verb lernen: remove –en to get stem lern–. Add the er/sie/es ending –t. lern + t = lernt. "Amara lernt jeden Tag Deutsch." The –t ending is used for er, sie, es, and for all names referring to one person: Amara, Kofi, Wanjiru, Fatima, Adesola.
exercises.rule_hint: er/sie/es → stem + –t. Amara lernt. Kofi macht. Wanjiru wohnt. Always –t for a single named person.

lessons.title: sein — the most important verb in German
lessons.content: sein (to be) is the most frequently used verb in German and the most irregular. There is no pattern to derive from the infinitive — you must memorise all six forms. The good news: they are short, distinctive, and you will use them in every Goethe task you ever write or speak.

exercises.question: "Kofi und Wanjiru ___ aus Nairobi." — Which form of sein?
exercises.hint: Kofi und Wanjiru = two people. Two people not including you = which pronoun?
exercises.explanation: "sind" — Kofi und Wanjiru are two people not including the speaker, which makes them sie (they). The sein form for sie (they) is sind. "Kofi und Wanjiru sind aus Nairobi." Remember: wir and sie share the same form — both use sind. When two or more people are the subject and you are not one of them, use sie/sind.
exercises.rule_hint: Two or more people (not including me) = sie (they) = sind. "Kofi und Wanjiru sind..." always takes sind.

lessons.title: haben & werden — two more essential irregulars

exercises.question: "Fatima ___ (haben) eine Schwester in Mombasa." Correct form?
exercises.hint: Fatima = she = er/sie/es. Watch what happens to the "b" in haben!
exercises.explanation: "hat" — Fatima is she (er/sie/es). The haben form for er/sie/es is hat. The "b" from haben disappears in the du and er/sie/es forms. Common error: using "habt" — but habt is only for ihr (you all). The pattern: ich habe → du hast → er/sie/es hat. "Fatima hat eine Schwester in Mombasa."
exercises.rule_hint: haben er/sie/es = hat. NOT habt (that is ihr-form only). The b disappears: habe → hast → hat.

lessons.title: The conjugation trap — why "er lerne" is wrong
lessons.content: The most common conjugation mistake in German is using the same verb form for every pronoun — writing "er lerne" just like "ich lerne". This happens because English verbs barely change shape, so the instinct is to treat the verb as fixed. German works completely differently: every single pronoun gets its own ending.

exercises.question: A student writes: "Er lerne Deutsch und du lerne auch." What is wrong?
exercises.hint: Think about how English verb forms compare to German — what does English do that German does not?
options: The word order is wrong — verb should be at the end
The same verb form "lerne" is used for all pronouns — each needs its own ending
The verb lernen cannot be used with er or du
There is no mistake — this is correct German
exercises.correct_answer: The same verb form "lerne" is used for all pronouns — each needs its own ending
exercises.explanation: The verb form does not change with the pronoun. "er lerne" should be "er lernt" (er/sie/es → stem + –t). "du lerne" should be "du lernst" (du → stem + –st). In English, the verb barely changes (I learn, you learn, he learns — only one –s). Learners carry over this instinct and use one form for all pronouns. In German, the ending changes for every pronoun without exception.
exercises.rule_hint: Each pronoun → its own ending. ich→–e · du→–st · er→–t. NEVER "er lerne" or "du lerne". Always "er lernt" and "du lernst".

lessons.title: African contexts — conjugation in real sentences
lessons.content: Let's put everything together using sentences from Nairobi, the market, and family life — the exact contexts that appear in Goethe A1 Schreiben and Sprechen tasks. Check every verb ending as you read.

exercises.question: Which sentence has ALL verb forms correct?
exercises.hint: Check every single verb ending carefully against its subject pronoun
options: Amara lernt in Nairobi und ihr Bruder Kofi studiert in Mombasa.
Amara lerne in Nairobi und ihr Bruder Kofi studiert in Mombasa.
Amara lernt in Nairobi und ihr Bruder Kofi studieren in Mombasa.
Amara lernen in Nairobi und ihr Bruder Kofi studiert in Mombasa.
exercises.correct_answer: Amara lernt in Nairobi und ihr Bruder Kofi studiert in Mombasa.
exercises.explanation: "Amara lernt in Nairobi und ihr Bruder Kofi studiert in Mombasa." — Amara = sie → lernt (stem lern– + –t). Kofi = er → studiert (stem studier– + –t). Both subjects are single named people (er/sie), so both verbs take –t. The other options each contain at least one wrong ending: "lerne" (ich-form wrongly used for sie), "studieren" (wir/sie-form wrongly used for er), "lernen" (wir/sie-form wrongly used for sie).
exercises.rule_hint: Names = er or sie = always –t ending. Check each subject separately. Amara lernt. Kofi studiert.

title: PersonalPronouns
description: Pronouns are the words that replace nouns — ich, du, er, sie, wir. Every German verb depends on knowing the right pronoun because the pronoun determines the verb ending. But German has one trap that catches almost every learner: the word sie has three completely different meanings. And using the wrong one with your Goethe examiner costs marks.

lessons.title: The complete German pronoun table
lessons.content: A personal pronoun replaces a noun so you do not have to repeat it. "Kwame lernt Deutsch — er lernt jeden Tag." German has nine personal pronouns, organised by person (1st / 2nd / 3rd) and number (singular / plural), with one extra level: formality.

exercises.question: Kwame und Amina lernen zusammen Deutsch. Which pronoun replaces "Kwame und Amina"?
exercises.hint: Two people not including the speaker — which 3rd person plural pronoun?
exercises.explanation: "sie" (they) — Kwame und Amina are two people not including the speaker, so we use the 3rd person plural pronoun sie. "Sie lernen zusammen Deutsch." Note: this sie (they) is spelled identically to sie (she). The verb form lernen (–en, plural) confirms this is "they" rather than "she" (which would need lernt with –t).
exercises.rule_hint: Two or more named people = sie (they) → verb takes –en. Kwame und Amina = sie = lernen.

lessons.title: The sie / sie / Sie trap
lessons.content: This is the single most confusing point in German grammar for almost every learner — and it costs exam points every day. Three pronouns, one spelling, three completely different meanings. Here is how to crack it. When "Sie/sie" starts a sentence — where everything is capitalised — you cannot use the capital-letter rule. Use the verb ending instead: –t means she, –en means they or formal Sie. Then use context to choose between those two.

exercises.question: Which sentence uses "Sie" to mean the formal "you" — not "she" or "they"?
exercises.hint: Look for a sentence where Sie is clearly addressed TO the listener with respect
options: Amina lernt Deutsch. Sie lernt jeden Tag.
Kofi und Fatima kommen aus Ghana. Sie kommen morgen an.
Sie hat einen Bruder in Lagos.
exercises.explanation: "Sind Sie die Prüferin?" — The verb-first structure (Sind) signals a yes/no question directed at the listener. The capital S mid-sentence and the professional context confirm formal Sie. Option A: "Sie lernt" — lernt has –t, so this is sie (she). Option B: "Sie kommen" — context (Kofi und Fatima were just mentioned) → they. Option D: "Sie hat" — hat has –t, so this is sie (she).
exercises.rule_hint: "Sind Sie...?" is the clearest signal of formal Sie — a direct respectful question. Capital S + –en verb + addressed to listener = formal Sie.

lessons.title: Pronouns and verb conjugation — the full picture
lessons.content: In Module 8 you learned the six verb endings. Now you can see exactly why there are six endings for nine pronouns: some pronouns share an ending. The pronoun determines the ending. Get the pronoun right and the ending follows automatically.

exercises.question: Fatima ___ (kommen) aus Lagos. Choose the correct verb form.
exercises.hint: Fatima = she. Which pronoun slot does she fall into? What ending does it take?
exercises.explanation: "kommt" — Fatima is she (sie, 3rd person singular). Regular verb kommen: remove –en to get stem komm–. Add the er/sie/es ending –t. komm + t = kommt. This is the same rule from Module 8: identify the pronoun → attach the right ending. Fatima = sie (she) = –t ending.
exercises.rule_hint: Fatima = sie (she) = er/sie/es slot = stem + –t. Fatima kommt. Amina lernt. Kofi wohnt.

lessons.title: Formal Sie — culture, respect, and the exam
lessons.content: German has two ways to say "you." du is for people you know well — family, friends, classmates. Sie is for everyone else: strangers, professionals, officials, people older than you in formal settings. The choice of du or Sie is not just grammar — it is a cultural statement about respect and social distance. The formal Sie always takes the same verb ending as wir and sie (they): –en for regular verbs. This applies to all tenses. The verb form is identical to the 3rd person plural.

exercises.question: You enter the Goethe exam room. The examiner greets you. Which sentence do YOU use to greet back correctly?
exercises.hint: The examiner is a professional stranger. Which level of formality does German require?
options: Guten Morgen. Sind Sie die Prüferin?
exercises.correct_answer: Guten Morgen. Sind Sie die Prüferin?
exercises.explanation: "Guten Morgen. Sind Sie die Prüferin?" — The examiner is a professional stranger. German requires Sie in all formal and professional contexts. Using Sie from the very first second shows cultural competence, which the Goethe exam assesses. "Hallo/Hi/Hey" with du or ihr sounds rude and signals a lack of register awareness. Sie + a formal greeting (Guten Morgen / Guten Tag) is the correct and expected opening.
exercises.rule_hint: Examiner = professional stranger = always Sie. Guten Morgen + Sind Sie...? is the correct formal exam opening.

lessons.title: The "du" mistake — why it costs you points
lessons.content: In many African cultures, greetings are warm and quickly informal — you might address an elder directly by name, or use the same register with everyone. German culture treats formality very differently. Using du with a stranger in a formal setting does not just sound informal — it sounds disrespectful. In a Goethe exam, it signals to the examiner that you have not mastered this key aspect of German language and culture.

exercises.question: A student says to the Goethe examiner: "Du sprichst sehr gut Deutsch." What is wrong?
exercises.hint: Think about the relationship between the student and the examiner — and what German culture expects
options: The word order is wrong — verb should be at the end
du is used instead of Sie — the examiner must always be addressed formally
sprichst is the wrong verb form for any pronoun
There is no problem — this sentence is correct
exercises.correct_answer: du is used instead of Sie — the examiner must always be addressed formally
exercises.explanation: "du" should be "Sie". The examiner is a professional in a formal assessment — in German culture, this is Sie territory without exception. "Du sprichst" should be "Sie sprechen" (Sie → –en ending, not –st). Using du signals either that you do not understand German social norms or have not learned the formal register. Both cost marks in Sprechen. The correct sentence: "Sie sprechen sehr gut Deutsch."
exercises.rule_hint: Examiner = Sie always. "Sie sprechen" not "du sprichst". Changing pronoun also changes the verb ending: du –st → Sie –en.

lessons.title: Kwame, Amina, Kofi, Fatima — pronouns in real sentences
lessons.content: Let us put all nine pronouns into realistic African-context sentences — the exact kind of content you describe in Goethe A1 Schreiben and Sprechen. Watch how names become pronouns and how pronouns drive verb endings.

exercises.question: "Kofi und Fatima ___ meine Freunde." Which form of sein completes the sentence?
exercises.hint: Kofi und Fatima = two people = which pronoun? Then check sein for that pronoun.
exercises.explanation: "sind" — Kofi und Fatima are two people not including the speaker → sie (they) → 3rd person plural. The sein conjugation for sie (they) is sind. "Kofi und Fatima sind meine Freunde." Remember from Module 8: sein is irregular. wir = sind, sie (they) = sind, Sie (formal) = sind — all three share the same form. seid is only for ihr.
exercises.rule_hint: Two or more people (3rd person plural) = sie (they) = sind. Kofi und Fatima sind... Always sind for a group of people you are talking about.

title: Plural Formsof Nouns
description: In English, almost everything gets an –s to become plural: book → books, car → cars, day → days. German does not work like that. German has five plural patterns and none of them are predictable just by looking at the singular. This module shows you every pattern, gives you the A1 exam vocabulary, and teaches you the one strategy that prevents every mistake.

lessons.title: Why German plurals are unpredictable
lessons.content: In English the plural rule is almost universal: add –s (or –es). Book → books. Car → cars. Day → days. You can guess an English plural you have never seen before and be right 95% of the time. German cannot be guessed. The same ending can appear on completely different nouns, and many nouns change their vowel entirely in the plural. There is no shortcut to predicting which pattern a noun follows — but there are tendencies you can learn. More importantly, there is one strategy that removes all guessing.

exercises.question: What article do ALL German plural nouns use, regardless of their singular article?
exercises.hint: Der Mann, das Kind, die Frau — what do they ALL become in the plural?
exercises.explanation: "die" — Every German plural noun uses the article die, without exception. Der Mann (masculine singular) → die Männer (plural). Das Kind (neuter singular) → die Kinder (plural). Die Frau (feminine singular) → die Frauen (plural). Whatever the singular article is, the plural is always die. This is the one consistent rule in the entire German plural system.
exercises.rule_hint: ALL plurals = die. Der/die/das → always die in the plural. No exceptions.

lessons.title: Pattern 1: No change · Pattern 2: +e
lessons.content: The singular and plural forms look completely identical. You can only tell them apart from the article: der Lehrer (one teacher) vs die Lehrer (multiple teachers). The article does all the work. Add –e to the end of the noun. Some nouns also add an umlaut (vowel change) on the main vowel, but many do not. This is the most common pattern for masculine nouns and many neuter nouns.

exercises.question: Kofi arbeitet jeden Tag. Er hat viele ___. (der Tag = day) Choose the correct plural.
exercises.hint: Tag is a masculine noun. Which of the five patterns does it follow? Does it add –e, –er, –en, or –s?
exercises.explanation: "die Tage" — Tag follows Pattern 2: add –e. The stem is unchanged (no umlaut for Tag) and –e is added: Tag + e = Tage. "die Tags" applies the English –s rule incorrectly to a native German noun. "die Täge" adds a wrong umlaut — Tag does not take one. "die Tagen" would be used only in the dative case, not as the standard plural form.
exercises.rule_hint: der Tag → die Tage (+e, no umlaut). Pattern 2 applies to most short masculine nouns: Tag, Beruf, Weg, Beruf. Remember the trio: der Tag / die Tage.

lessons.title: Pattern 3: +er · Pattern 4: +en/+n
lessons.content: Add –er to the stem. Nouns with the vowels a, o, u, or au almost always add an umlaut as well. Pattern 3 is most common with short neuter nouns, but also appears with some masculine nouns like der Mann. This is the most common pattern for feminine nouns. If a noun ends in a consonant, add –en. If a noun already ends in –e, just add –n. A special rule applies to feminine nouns ending in –in: they add –nen.

exercises.question: Amina hat drei ___. (das Kind = child) Choose the correct plural.
exercises.hint: Kind is a neuter noun. Short neuter nouns often follow which pattern?
exercises.explanation: "die Kinder" — Kind follows Pattern 3: add –er. The stem stays unchanged (i does not take an umlaut) and –er is added: Kind + er = Kinder. "die Kinds" applies the English –s rule to a native German noun — wrong. "die Kinde" would be Pattern 2 (+e) — also wrong for Kind. "die Kinden" is not a real pattern in German.
exercises.rule_hint: das Kind → die Kinder (+er, no umlaut). Pattern 3 (+er) is most common with short neuter nouns. Remember: Kind, Bild, Lied all follow +er.

lessons.title: Pattern 5: +s · and the English trap
lessons.content: Add –s to the end of the noun — exactly like English. But this is the minority pattern in German. It applies mainly to foreign loanwords, words ending in a vowel that is not –e, and abbreviations. Only about 5–10% of German nouns use this pattern. Because English uses –s for almost everything, learners naturally want to do the same in German. This is one of the most common and most clearly wrong mistakes a learner can make. Native German nouns never use –s in the plural.

exercises.question: Which of these plural forms is correct German?
exercises.hint: Think about which nouns use the –s pattern and which never do.
exercises.explanation: "die Schulen" is correct — die Schule follows Pattern 4 (+n), giving die Schulen. The other three options all add –s to native German nouns, which is never correct. "die Frauns", "die Männs", and "die Kinds" do not exist in German. The –s pattern only applies to foreign loanwords like Auto, Hotel, Café, and Handy — not to core German vocabulary.
exercises.rule_hint: The –s plural only applies to foreign loanwords. Native German nouns NEVER use –s. die Schule → die Schulen (Pattern 4, +n).

lessons.title: The golden rule in practice
lessons.content: You now know all five patterns. The question is: how do you remember which noun uses which pattern? The answer is the golden rule in action: never learn a noun without its plural. Treat the trio as one unit of vocabulary, not as three separate things. Pattern 4 (+en/+n) covers nearly half of all German nouns because it handles almost all feminine nouns. Knowing this guides your guessing when you have not memorised a plural yet.

exercises.question: Fatima says: "Ich habe zwei Manns gesehen." What is wrong with this sentence?
exercises.hint: Think about which plural pattern der Mann uses — and whether –s ever works on native German nouns.
options: "Manns" should be "Männer" — Mann takes Pattern 3 (+er with umlaut a→ä)
Nothing is wrong — this sentence is correct
"zwei" is wrong — use "ein paar" with plural nouns
exercises.correct_answer: "Manns" should be "Männer" — Mann takes Pattern 3 (+er with umlaut a→ä)
exercises.explanation: "Manns" does not exist. der Mann follows Pattern 3: add –er and change a → ä. Mann → Männ + er = Männer. The correct sentence is: "Ich habe zwei Männer gesehen." The –s ending on a native German noun like Mann is completely wrong and immediately recognisable as an error. Never apply the English –s rule to native German nouns, even when guessing.
exercises.rule_hint: der Mann → die Männer (+er with umlaut: a→ä). Never "die Manns". The –s pattern is for loanwords only: Autos, Hotels, Cafés, Handys.

lessons.title: Kwame, Amina, Kofi, Fatima — all five patterns
lessons.content: Every pattern you have learned now appears in African-context sentences. Notice how the plural article is always die regardless of the singular article — that rule never changes.

exercises.question: Kwame kauft drei ___. (das Buch = book) Choose the correct plural form.
exercises.hint: Buch is a neuter noun with the vowel u. Pattern 3 (+er) often changes vowels — what happens to u?
exercises.explanation: "die Bücher" — das Buch follows Pattern 3: add –er, and the vowel u changes to ü (umlaut). Buch → Büch + er = Bücher. "die Buchs" applies the English –s rule to a native German noun — wrong. "die Buche" is actually a German word but it means "beech tree", not the plural of Buch. "die Büche" is not a valid form. The trio is: das Buch / die Bücher.
exercises.rule_hint: das Buch → die Bücher (+er with umlaut: u→ü). Same pattern: das Haus → die Häuser, das Buch → die Bücher. Always learn as a trio.

title: Negation:nicht and kein
description: English has one word for "not" — and it goes right after the verb. German has two completely different negation words, each with its own rules, and neither of them works exactly like English "not." This module gives you the decision logic, the grammar, the word order rules, and the exam strategy — so that negation becomes free points in your Goethe A1 Schreiben.

lessons.title: nicht vs kein — the core rule
lessons.content: English speakers learning German face an immediate problem: English has one negation word — "not" — and it goes in one predictable place (right after "be" or with "do"). German has two negation words that work differently from each other and differently from English "not." The good news: the decision between them follows one clear rule.

exercises.question: Kofi möchte ein Auto kaufen, aber er hat ___ Auto. (Kofi would like to buy a car but he has no car.)
exercises.hint: The positive sentence uses "ein Auto". What do you use to negate a noun with ein?
exercises.explanation: "kein" — Auto is neuter (das Auto). In the positive sentence you would say "er hat ein Auto." To negate, you replace ein with kein. Neuter nouns in the accusative case take kein (no –en, no –e). The full sentence: "Er hat kein Auto." Using "nicht ein" is a direct translation error from English — it is wrong in German.
exercises.rule_hint: Noun with ein/eine → replace with kein. das Auto (neuter accusative) → kein Auto. Never "nicht ein".

lessons.title: kein — forms, genders, cases
lessons.content: kein is not just one word — it changes form based on the gender of the noun it negates and the grammatical case. It follows the same pattern as ein/eine. If you know how ein changes, you already know how kein changes. When you negate a profession or identity after sein (to be), use kein — even though there is no article in the positive sentence. German treats profession nouns after sein as if they had an indefinite article.

exercises.question: Amina hat ___ Bruder. (Amina has no brother.) Choose the correct form of kein.
exercises.hint: Bruder = masculine (der Bruder). It follows haben, so it is in the accusative case. What is kein in masculine accusative?
exercises.explanation: "keinen" — Bruder is masculine (der Bruder). After haben, the noun is in the accusative case. Masculine accusative is the only form of kein that changes: kein → keinen. All other genders stay the same in accusative. "kein Bruder" would be nominative masculine (correct in other contexts), but after haben we need accusative → keinen. "keine" is for feminine and plural. "nicht" would be wrong here — you never use nicht to negate a noun with ein.
exercises.rule_hint: Masculine noun after haben = accusative = keinen. Only masculine accusative changes. der Bruder → keinen Bruder. die Schwester → keine Schwester. das Kind → kein Kind.

lessons.title: nicht — what it negates
lessons.content: nicht is the general negation word. It negates everything that is not a noun with an indefinite or no article: verbs, predicative adjectives, adverbs, proper nouns, and also definite articles (the, this). Learn the categories and the position rule will follow naturally.

exercises.question: Kofi ist ___ müde. (Kofi is not tired.) Which word completes this sentence?
exercises.hint: müde is an adjective. Are you negating a noun with ein/eine — or are you negating an adjective?
exercises.explanation: "nicht" — müde is an adjective, not a noun. kein only negates nouns (words that could have ein/eine or no article). To negate an adjective after sein, you always use nicht. Position: nicht goes directly before the adjective — "Kofi ist nicht müde." Never use kein before an adjective — "kein müde" does not exist in German.
exercises.rule_hint: Adjective after sein → nicht. Ich bin nicht müde. Kofi ist nicht krank. Das Wetter ist nicht gut. kein only works with nouns.

lessons.title: Position of nicht in the sentence
lessons.content: The biggest nicht question after "which word?" is "where does it go?" Unlike English "not" which sits immediately after the auxiliary verb, German nicht moves around depending on what it is negating. There are three clear positions to learn. When you want to negate the entire sentence (the whole action or event), nicht goes to the end of the clause — after all other elements. When the sentence has sein + a predicative adjective, nicht goes directly before that adjective. Because the adjective is already at the end, nicht ends up near the end too — but it comes before the adjective, not after. When you want to negate a specific word or phrase — not the whole sentence — nicht goes directly before the element it negates. Whatever word comes immediately after nicht is what is being denied.

exercises.question: Which sentence places nicht in the correct position?
exercises.hint: Think about the rule: nicht never goes between subject and verb. Which option puts it in the right place?
exercises.explanation: "Ich bin nicht müde." — Option C is correct: sein + adjective → nicht goes directly before the adjective. Option A puts nicht between subject and verb (Ich / nicht / arbeite) — this is never correct. Option B also puts nicht between subject and verb (Ich / nicht / bin) — wrong for the same reason. Option D scrambles the word order entirely. The rule: nicht never separates subject from the conjugated verb.
exercises.rule_hint: nicht never goes between subject and conjugated verb. Sein + adjective: nicht comes before the adjective. Ich bin nicht müde. Er ist nicht krank.

lessons.title: The English trap and exam tips
lessons.content: English-speaking learners make two very predictable negation errors. The first is using nicht where kein is needed. The second is placing nicht in the English position — immediately after the verb — instead of near the end. Both errors appear in every A1 exam script from learners who have not studied this module. English: "I do not have a car" → Word-for-word German: "Ich habe nicht ein Auto" — WRONG. When the positive sentence uses ein/eine, you must replace it with kein. You cannot keep the article and add nicht before it. These are actually correct because sein + adjective puts nicht before the adjective (near the end), and "Ich komme nicht" has nicht at the very end. The only wrong pattern is subject + verb + nicht when the verb is NOT sein: "Ich arbeite nicht heute" ✓ (nicht before heute) vs "Ich nicht arbeite" ✗ (subject–verb split).

exercises.question: Fatima says: "Ich habe nicht ein Handy." What is wrong with this sentence?
exercises.hint: The positive sentence would be "Ich habe ein Handy." What rule applies when negating a noun with ein?
options: "nicht ein" should be "kein" — kein replaces ein when negating a noun
The word order is wrong — nicht should come first
There is no error — this sentence is correct
exercises.correct_answer: "nicht ein" should be "kein" — kein replaces ein when negating a noun
exercises.explanation: "nicht ein" should be "kein". The positive sentence is "Ich habe ein Handy." To negate, you replace ein with kein: "Ich habe kein Handy." You cannot keep ein and add nicht in front of it — "nicht ein" is a direct word-for-word translation from English and does not exist as correct German. This error costs marks in every A1 Schreiben task where negation appears. The correct form: "Ich habe kein Handy."
exercises.rule_hint: "nicht ein" is never correct. Replace ein/eine with kein/keine/keinen. Ich habe ein Handy → Ich habe kein Handy. Always kein, never nicht + ein.

lessons.title: Kwame, Amina, Kofi, Fatima — full negation review
lessons.content: Every negation rule you have learned now appears in African-context sentences. For each sentence, notice which word is used (nicht or kein) and where it sits in the sentence.

exercises.question: Kwame ist ___ Arzt. (Kwame is not a doctor.) Choose the correct word.
exercises.hint: Arzt = a profession noun after sein. In the positive sentence: "Kwame ist Arzt" — no article. What do you use to negate a noun (even without article) after sein?
exercises.explanation: "kein Arzt" — profession nouns after sein use kein for negation, even though the positive sentence has no article ("Kwame ist Arzt"). German treats these nouns as if they had an invisible ein: "Kwame ist [ein] Arzt" → "Kwame ist kein Arzt." "nicht Arzt" is wrong — nicht does not negate nouns. "keinen Arzt" would be accusative masculine (after haben), but after sein we use nominative → kein. "kein Ärzte" is wrong because Ärzte is plural (die Ärzte) and should use keine.
exercises.rule_hint: Profession/identity after sein → kein for negation. Kwame ist Arzt → Kwame ist kein Arzt. Fatima ist Lehrerin → Fatima ist keine Lehrerin. Nominative case after sein.

title: ModalVerbs
description: Six small verbs that transform every sentence you can build. "I work" becomes "I can work", "I must work", "I want to work", "I would like to work." Modal verbs are the engine of the Goethe A1 Sprechen section — examiners expect you to use them to express ability, obligation, desire, and polite requests.

lessons.title: The six modal verbs
lessons.content: A modal verb expresses the relationship between the subject and an action — ability, obligation, permission, desire. In English you use "can", "must", "want to", "may". German has six modal verbs, each with a precise meaning. Together they cover almost everything you will need in the Goethe A1 Sprechen section.

exercises.question: Which modal verb expresses that you are ALLOWED to do something — permission?
exercises.hint: Think: "May I...?" or "You are not allowed to..." — which modal covers permission and prohibition?
exercises.explanation: "dürfen" expresses permission — what you are allowed or not allowed to do. "Hier darf man nicht rauchen" = smoking is not allowed here. "Darf ich fragen?" = May I ask? können expresses ability (can do), müssen expresses necessity (have to), sollen expresses obligation from another person (supposed to). dürfen is the permission modal.
exercises.rule_hint: dürfen = permission / allowed to. Hier darf man parken. Ich darf das nicht machen. The negative "darf nicht" = is not allowed.

lessons.title: Conjugating modal verbs
lessons.content: Modal verbs have irregular stems in the ich / er / sie / es forms — the vowel often changes from the infinitive. But there is a pattern: the ich form and the er/sie/es form are identical — no ending is added. This is different from every other German verb. The other forms (du, wir, ihr, sie/Sie) follow more regular endings.

exercises.question: Amina ___ heute zur Schule gehen. (Amina has to go to school today.) Which form is correct?
exercises.hint: The subject is Amina = she = er/sie/es. Which modal means "have to"? What is its er/sie/es form?
exercises.explanation: "muss" — müssen is the correct modal (have to / must). Amina = she = er/sie/es conjugation. The er/sie/es form of müssen is muss (vowel changes: ü → u, no –t ending added — remember: ich/er/sie/es forms of modals have no ending). "müssen" is the infinitive — wrong here. "musst" is the du form. "kann" means can, not must.
exercises.rule_hint: müssen → ich muss / er muss / sie muss. Vowel change: ü → u. No –t ending for ich/er/sie/es. Amina = sie = muss.

lessons.title: Sentence structure: verb goes to the end
lessons.content: This is the single most important structural rule for modal verbs — and the rule that costs African learners the most marks when they forget it. When you use a modal verb in a sentence, the modal takes position 2 (right after the subject) and the main verb moves to the very end of the sentence as an infinitive (the base form ending in –en). The most common error is placing the infinitive immediately after the modal — following the English word order: "I can speak German." In English the main verb stays near the modal. In German it must jump to the end. When the meaning is obvious from context, the infinitive can be dropped. This is common with können and with movement verbs.

exercises.question: Which sentence has the CORRECT word order with a modal verb?
exercises.hint: Remember: modal verb in position 2, main verb as infinitive at the END.
exercises.explanation: "Ich kann gut Deutsch sprechen." — This is the only correct option. Structure: Ich (subject, pos.1) + kann (modal, pos.2) + gut Deutsch (middle — adverb + object) + sprechen (infinitive at END). Option A puts sprechen immediately after kann — wrong. Option B also puts sprechen in the wrong position. Option C moves the modal away from position 2 — wrong. Only D follows the correct modal sentence structure.
exercises.rule_hint: MODAL STRUCTURE: Subject + MODAL(pos.2) + [middle] + INFINITIVE(END). Ich kann gut Deutsch sprechen. The infinitive always goes to the very end.

lessons.title: möchten — the exam superword
lessons.content: Ich möchte is arguably the single most useful phrase in the entire Goethe A1 Sprechen section. It is polite, it is flexible, and it answers a vast range of examiner questions. If you are unsure what to say, start with "Ich möchte" and finish the sentence with an infinitive. You will almost always produce a correct, appropriate, mark-earning response. möchten can also be used without an infinitive when the meaning is clear from context — particularly for ordering food/drinks or making requests for objects.

exercises.question: Fatima is in a Goethe A1 Sprechen exam. The examiner asks: "Was möchten Sie nach dem Deutschkurs machen?" Which answer is best?
exercises.hint: Use möchten for a polite response. Where does the infinitive go?
exercises.explanation: "Ich möchte in Deutschland arbeiten." — This is correct on all counts: polite register (möchten not wollen), modal in position 2 (möchte), infinitive at the END (arbeiten). Option A uses wollen — too direct for exam register, and the word order is wrong (arbeiten not at end). Option C puts arbeiten before "in Deutschland" — the infinitive must come last. Option D puts the modal at the end instead of position 2 — wrong structure.
exercises.rule_hint: Exam formula: Ich möchte + [context/object] + INFINITIVE(END). Ich möchte in Deutschland arbeiten. Infinitive always last.

lessons.title: Common mistakes and fixes
lessons.content: The most frequent error. Learners place the infinitive immediately after the modal, following English word order. The infinitive must be at the end. Learners sometimes use the infinitive form for ich or er/sie/es, forgetting the vowel change. müssen = internal necessity or strong obligation (you have to / must). sollen = obligation from another person (you are supposed to / someone told you to).

exercises.question: Kofi says: "Der Lehrer hat gesagt, ich soll morgen früh kommen." What does sollen express here?
exercises.hint: Think about who is creating the obligation — is it Kofi himself or someone else?
options: Kofi wants to come early tomorrow
Kofi has to come because of an internal reason
The teacher told Kofi to come — obligation from another person
Kofi is able to come early
exercises.correct_answer: The teacher told Kofi to come — obligation from another person
exercises.explanation: "sollen" expresses an obligation that comes from another person — in this case, the teacher told Kofi to come early. sollen = "supposed to / has been told to." This is the key difference from müssen: müssen = internal necessity or strong force of circumstance; sollen = instruction from another person. "Der Lehrer hat gesagt" (the teacher said) is the signal that this is sollen, not müssen.
exercises.rule_hint: sollen = obligation from another person. Someone told you to do it. müssen = internal necessity or external force. Der Lehrer sagt → sollen. Der Zug fährt ab → müssen.

lessons.title: Kwame, Amina, Kofi, Fatima — all 6 modals
lessons.content: All six modal verbs appear below in African-context sentences. For each, the modal is in position 2 and the infinitive is at the end. Notice the conjugation and the structure.

exercises.question: Complete the sentence: "Kwame ___ drei Sprachen ___ ." (Kwame can speak three languages.) Choose the correctly structured option.
exercises.hint: können conjugated for Kwame (= er). Main verb sprechen goes to the end.
exercises.explanation: "kann / sprechen" — Kwame = er/sie/es → the correct form of können is kann (no ending, vowel change). The main verb sprechen stays as an infinitive and goes to the end: "Kwame kann drei Sprachen sprechen." "spricht" is the conjugated form of sprechen for er — but with a modal, the main verb must stay as an infinitive. "können" is the infinitive/wir/sie form, not the er form. "will" changes the meaning to "wants to".
exercises.rule_hint: können → ich/er/sie/es = kann. Main verb always stays as infinitive at end. Kwame kann drei Sprachen sprechen.

title: PerfektTense
description: Welcome to A2. The Perfekt tense is the most important grammar topic in the entire Goethe A2 exam — it appears in every single Schreiben task. Germans use Perfekt for almost all spoken and written past events, the way English speakers use "I went", "I ate", "I worked". Master this and you unlock the ability to talk about your life, your experiences, and your journey to Germany.

lessons.title: What is Perfekt — and why Germans use it
lessons.content: English has many ways to express the past: "I went", "I have gone", "I was going", "I had gone." German simplifies this. For everyday conversation and most written tasks, Germans use Perfekt — one tense that covers what happened. There is another past tense called Präteritum (simple past), but it is mainly used in formal writing, novels, and news. When a German says "What did you do yesterday?" and you answer, you use Perfekt. When you write an email in your Goethe exam, you use Perfekt. Just like modal verbs, Perfekt has a two-part verb system. The auxiliary (haben/sein) sits in position 2. The past participle goes to the end of the sentence. Most African English speakers translate from English simple past directly into German: "I went" → "Ich ging." This is Präteritum. It is grammatically correct but sounds formal and strange in conversation and standard exam writing. Use Perfekt instead.

exercises.question: Kofi wants to say "Kofi ate rice" in German Perfekt. Which sentence is correct?
exercises.hint: Perfekt uses two parts: auxiliary in position 2, participle at the end. essen is irregular.
exercises.explanation: "Kofi hat Reis gegessen." — Correct Perfekt: haben auxiliary (hat) in position 2, irregular participle gegessen at the END. Option A is Präteritum (simple past) — avoid this in the exam. Option C uses sein — essen is not movement or change of state, so sein is wrong. Option D puts the participle before the object — the participle must go to the absolute end.
exercises.rule_hint: Perfekt structure: SUBJECT + haben/sein (position 2) + [middle] + PARTICIPLE (END). Kofi hat Reis gegessen.

lessons.title: Regular verbs: ge + stem + t
lessons.content: Regular verbs (also called "weak verbs") follow a completely predictable pattern. Take the infinitive, remove –en, then wrap it: add ge– at the front and –t at the end. That's it.

exercises.question: What is the correct Perfekt participle for "kaufen" (to buy)?
exercises.hint: kaufen is a regular verb. Apply the formula: ge + stem + t.
exercises.explanation: "gekauft" — kaufen is a regular (weak) verb. Formula: remove –en from infinitive → kauf. Add ge– at front and –t at end → gekauft. Option A keeps the –en ending — regular verbs end in –t not –en. Option B is Präteritum form. Option D has a double k — never double the consonant when adding ge–.
exercises.rule_hint: Regular Perfekt formula: ge + STEM + t. kaufen → kauf → ge+kauf+t = gekauft. Always –t ending for regular verbs.

lessons.title: Irregular verbs: ge + changed stem + en
lessons.content: Irregular verbs (also called "strong verbs") change their stem vowel and end in –en instead of –t. There is no formula for the stem change — you have to learn the common ones. The good news: the most important ones repeat constantly in exam tasks, so they become automatic quickly.

exercises.question: Which sentence correctly says "Amina read a book" in Perfekt?
exercises.hint: lesen is irregular. Check the participle list: lesen → gelesen. Which auxiliary does lesen use?
exercises.explanation: "Amina hat ein Buch gelesen." — lesen is irregular: ge+les+en = gelesen. It uses haben (reading a book = acting on an object, not movement). Option A uses sein — lesen is not movement or change of state, so sein is wrong. Option C "gelest" applies the regular –t ending to an irregular verb — lesen is irregular and ends in –en. Option D puts the infinitive instead of the participle.
exercises.rule_hint: lesen is irregular: gelesen. Uses haben. Amina hat ein Buch gelesen. Irregular participles end in –en, not –t.

lessons.title: haben vs sein — The Golden Rule
lessons.content: This is the biggest source of confusion in German Perfekt — and the most tested element in the Goethe A2 exam. Every Perfekt sentence uses either haben or sein as the auxiliary. Get this right and you will score heavily in Schreiben.

exercises.question: Kwame flew from Lagos to Berlin. Which sentence is correct?
exercises.hint: fliegen = movement from one place to another. Which auxiliary do we use for movement? fliegen is irregular.
options: Kwame hat von Lagos nach Berlin geflogen.
Kwame ist von Lagos nach Berlin geflogen.
Kwame ist von Lagos nach Berlin gefliegt.
Kwame hat von Lagos nach Berlin fliegen.
exercises.correct_answer: Kwame ist von Lagos nach Berlin geflogen.
exercises.explanation: "Kwame ist von Lagos nach Berlin geflogen." — fliegen = movement from A to B → uses sein (the Golden Rule). Irregular participle: ge+flog+en = geflogen. Option A uses haben — wrong for movement verbs. Option C "gefliegt" applies the regular –t ending to an irregular verb. Option D uses the infinitive instead of the participle.
exercises.rule_hint: Golden Rule: movement / change of state → sein. fliegen (to fly) = movement → sein. Irregular: geflogen. Kwame ist geflogen.

lessons.title: Separable verbs in Perfekt
lessons.content: Separable verbs are verbs with a detachable prefix: aufmachen, anrufen, einkaufen, abfahren. In present tense the prefix detaches to the end. In Perfekt, the ge– goes between the prefix and the stem — not before everything.

exercises.question: What is the correct Perfekt participle for the separable verb "anrufen" (to call)?
exercises.hint: Separable verbs: prefix + ge + stem + t/en. anrufen is irregular (rufen → gerufen).
exercises.explanation: "angerufen" — anrufen is separable: prefix is an–, stem is ruf–, it is irregular (en ending). Formula: an + ge + ruf + en = angerufen. Option A "geangekommen" confuses anrufen with ankommen and puts ge at the front — never put ge before the whole separable verb. Option C puts the prefix at the end — wrong. Option D is a made-up Präteritum form.
exercises.rule_hint: Separable verb Perfekt: PREFIX + ge + STEM + t/en. anrufen → an+ge+ruf+en = angerufen. The ge always goes between the prefix and the stem.

lessons.title: Kwame, Amina, Kofi, Fatima — Perfekt in context
lessons.content: Six Perfekt sentences in African situations. Each sentence is marked to show: the auxiliary (haben or sein), the reason for the choice, and the participle type. Read, listen, and internalise the pattern.

exercises.question: Complete: "Fatima ___ gestern in die Bibliothek ___ ." (Fatima went to the library yesterday.)
exercises.hint: gehen = movement from one place to another. Which auxiliary? gehen is irregular: gegangen.
exercises.explanation: "ist / gegangen" — gehen = movement → sein. The er/sie/es form of sein is ist. Irregular participle: ge+gang+en = gegangen. Option A uses hat — haben is wrong for movement verbs. Option C "gegehen" keeps the original stem — the stem changes: geh → gang. Option D "gegangt" incorrectly applies the regular –t ending to an irregular verb.
exercises.rule_hint: gehen = movement → sein. sein conjugation: ich bin / du bist / er/sie/es ist / wir sind. Irregular participle: gegangen. Fatima ist gegangen.

title: Separable &Inseparable Verbs
description: German verbs can be extended with prefixes that completely change their meaning. The critical distinction: some prefixes separate from the verb in a sentence (sending the prefix to the end), while others stay permanently attached. Both types appear constantly in the Goethe A2 Hören and Lesen tasks — and getting word order wrong in separable verbs is one of the most penalised errors in A2 Schreiben.

lessons.title: What are separable verbs?
lessons.content: A separable verb is a verb built from a base verb + a short prefix. The prefix adds a specific meaning. When you use the verb in a sentence, the two parts split: the base verb stays in position 2, and the prefix jumps to the very end of the sentence. Think of it like a sentence with a delayed punch — the prefix arrives last and completes the meaning.

exercises.question: "aufmachen" is a separable verb. Which sentence correctly uses it to mean "Kwame opens the window"?
exercises.hint: Separable verbs split: the stem goes to position 2, the prefix goes to the very end.
exercises.explanation: "Kwame macht das Fenster auf." — Correct split: stem machen conjugated as macht in position 2, object das Fenster in the middle, prefix auf at the very end. Option A "aufmacht" keeps the prefix attached — the verb must split. Option B puts "auf" before the object — the prefix must go to the END of the whole sentence. Option D double-counts the prefix.
exercises.rule_hint: Separable verb structure: SUBJECT + STEM(pos.2) + [middle: objects, adverbs] + PREFIX(end). Kwame macht das Fenster auf.

lessons.title: Word order — prefix to the end
lessons.content: In a main clause, the stem of the separable verb goes to position 2 (right after the subject, as always), and the prefix travels to the very last position in the sentence. Everything else — the object, adverbs, prepositional phrases — stays in the middle.

exercises.question: Amina is going to the market. How does "einkaufen" correctly split in this sentence: "Amina ___ auf dem Markt ___"?
exercises.hint: einkaufen → stem is kaufen, prefix is ein. Conjugate for sie (Amina).
exercises.explanation: "kauft / ein" — einkaufen splits: stem kaufen conjugated for sie = kauft goes to position 2, prefix ein goes to the end. Full sentence: "Amina kauft auf dem Markt ein." Option A uses the unsplit form as if it were not separable. Option C puts the whole "kauft ein" in position 2 — the prefix cannot stay in position 2. Option D reverses the order entirely.
exercises.rule_hint: einkaufen → kauft (pos.2) … ein (end). Amina kauft auf dem Markt ein. The adverbial phrase "auf dem Markt" stays in the middle.

lessons.title: Inseparable verbs — prefixes that never leave
lessons.content: Some prefixes are permanently glued to the verb. They never separate, no matter the sentence type. The verb always moves as one complete unit to position 2 and stays there. These are called inseparable verbs (untrennbare Verben). There are eight inseparable prefixes. If you see any of these at the start of a verb, you know it will never split:

exercises.question: Which of these verbs is INSEPARABLE — meaning it will never split in a sentence?
exercises.hint: Look at the prefix. Inseparable prefixes: be, ge, er, ver, zer, ent, emp, miss.
exercises.explanation: "verstehen" is inseparable. Its prefix is ver–, which is one of the eight inseparable prefixes (be, ge, er, ver, zer, ent, emp, miss). "aufmachen" has prefix auf– which is separable. "anrufen" has prefix an– which is separable. "einkaufen" has prefix ein– which is separable. Only ver– is an inseparable prefix in this list.
exercises.rule_hint: Inseparable prefixes: be, ge, er, ver, zer, ent, emp, miss. Any verb starting with these never splits. verstehen → ich verstehe (never splits).

lessons.title: Separable verbs in Perfekt
lessons.content: In Module 13 you learned that regular Perfekt adds ge– at the front of the stem. For separable verbs, the ge– goes between the prefix and the stem — not before the whole word. Inseparable verbs never add ge– to their Perfekt participle. The inseparable prefix acts as the ge– equivalent.

exercises.question: What is the correct Perfekt form of the separable verb "anrufen"?
exercises.hint: Separable Perfekt formula: PREFIX + ge + STEM + t/en. anrufen is irregular (rufen → gerufen).
exercises.explanation: "angerufen" — anrufen is separable and irregular. Formula: prefix an + ge + stem ruf + irregular ending en = angerufen. Option A confuses anrufen with ankommen. Option B is a made-up Präteritum form. Option D puts the prefix at the end of the participle — in a participle the prefix comes FIRST (an-ge-rufen), not last.
exercises.rule_hint: Separable Perfekt: PREFIX + ge + STEM + t/en. anrufen → an + ge + ruf + en = angerufen. Prefix always comes first in the participle.

lessons.title: Separable verbs with modal verbs
lessons.content: When a separable verb is used together with a modal verb, the separable verb appears at the end of the sentence as an infinitive — and it stays together as one word. The prefix does not separate because the whole separable verb is being used as an infinitive.

exercises.question: Kofi wants to call his mother. Which sentence with a modal verb is correct?
exercises.hint: Modal verbs send the infinitive to the end. With separable verbs, the infinitive stays together as one word.
options: Kofi will rufen seine Mutter an.
exercises.explanation: "Kofi will seine Mutter anrufen." — With a modal, the separable verb goes to the end as a complete infinitive: anrufen (not split). Modal will in position 2. Object seine Mutter in the middle. Infinitive anrufen at the END. Option A splits anrufen even with the modal — wrong. Option B puts the infinitive before the object — infinitive must go to the very end. Option D is meaningless.
exercises.rule_hint: Modal + separable verb: SUBJECT + MODAL(pos.2) + [middle] + SEPARABLE-INFINITIVE(end). Kofi will seine Mutter anrufen. The infinitive stays whole.

lessons.title: Kwame, Amina, Kofi, Fatima — all patterns in context
lessons.content: All four patterns you have learned — present tense split, questions, Perfekt, and modal verbs — demonstrated in sentences set in African and German daily life.

exercises.question: Which sentence correctly says "Fatima has visited her family" using Perfekt?
exercises.hint: besuchen has prefix be– which is inseparable. Inseparable verbs do NOT add ge– in Perfekt. Uses haben.
exercises.explanation: "Fatima hat ihre Familie besucht." — besuchen is inseparable (be–). Inseparable verbs: (1) use haben as auxiliary (besuchen is not movement), (2) do NOT add ge– to the participle. besucht = the plain Perfekt participle without ge–. Option A uses sein — wrong auxiliary for besuchen. Option B adds ge– to an inseparable verb: "gebesucht" is incorrect. Option D adds –et instead of –t: the stem is besuch, participle is besucht.
exercises.rule_hint: Inseparable verbs in Perfekt: NO ge– added. besuchen → besucht (not gebesucht). Fatima hat ihre Familie besucht.

title: AdjectiveEndings
description: In English, adjectives never change: "a tall man", "a tall woman", "a tall child" — the same word every time. German is fundamentally different. Adjectives change their ending depending on the gender of the noun, the case (nominative/accusative/dative), and the article that comes before them. This is the biggest grammar shock for African English speakers entering A2.

lessons.title: Why do adjective endings change?
lessons.content: In English, adjectives are completely fixed. Whether you say "a good man", "a good woman", or "a good child" — the adjective "good" never changes. This is how all African English speakers learn adjectives: one word, one form, always. German works entirely differently. The adjective changes its ending based on three things: 1) the grammatical gender of the noun (masculine/feminine/neuter), 2) the case (nominative/accusative/dative), and 3) what article comes before it (der/die/das, ein/eine, or nothing).

exercises.question: In English, "a good man" and "a good woman" both use "good". What happens in German?
exercises.hint: Think about what changes with gender in German adjectives.
options: Both use "gut" — German works exactly like English
Both use "guten" — German adjectives always take -en
ein guter Mann vs eine gute Frau — the ending changes with gender
ein gut Mann vs eine gut Frau — adjectives never change
exercises.correct_answer: ein guter Mann vs eine gute Frau — the ending changes with gender
exercises.explanation: "ein guter Mann" vs "eine gute Frau" — German adjective endings change based on the gender and case of the noun and the article before it. After ein + masculine nominative → -er. After eine + feminine nominative → -e. This is the fundamental difference from English.
exercises.rule_hint: German adjectives change ending based on: 1) article type (definite/indefinite/none), 2) gender (masc/fem/neut), 3) case (nom/acc/dat). English adjectives never change.

lessons.title: After der / die / das — weak endings
lessons.content: When the definite article comes before the adjective, the article already carries the full gender and case signal. The adjective is "weak" — it just adds -e or -en. You never need -er, -es, or -em after der/die/das forms. The accusative case is used for the direct object of a sentence. For masculine nouns, der → den, and the adjective ending changes from -e to -en. For feminine and neuter nouns, the accusative is the same as nominative.

exercises.question: Fill in: "Der ___ Mann kommt aus Ghana." (alt — old)
exercises.hint: After a definite article (der) in nominative masculine, the adjective ending is weak.
exercises.explanation: "Der alte Mann" — after der (definite article, masculine nominative), the adjective takes the weak ending -e. The article der already signals "masculine nominative", so the adjective is lazy and adds -e. Not -er (that is for after ein), not -en (that is for accusative masculine or dative).
exercises.rule_hint: After der/die/das + nominative: adjective ending = -e. Der alte Mann, die alte Frau, das alte Kind.

lessons.title: After ein / eine — mixed endings
lessons.content: The indefinite article ein is a problem: it is the same form for both masculine AND neuter nouns in the nominative case. There is no way to tell from "ein" alone whether the noun is masculine or neuter. So the adjective must step up and carry that information itself — this is called the mixed declension.

exercises.question: Kwame has a good job. How do you say this in German? (gut, Job = masculine)
exercises.hint: Job is masculine. "a" = ein. Check the case (nominative after haben? No — it is accusative here).
exercises.explanation: "Kwame hat einen guten Job." — "hat" takes the accusative case for the object. Job is masculine. Accusative masculine indefinite article = einen. After einen (accusative masculine) → adjective ending is -en. Even after ein-forms, accusative masculine always gives -en.
exercises.rule_hint: Accusative masculine after indefinite article: einen + adjective + -en. Kwame hat einen guten Job. The -er form only appears in NOMINATIVE masculine after ein.

lessons.title: Dative case — always -en
lessons.content: The dative case is used after certain prepositions and with indirect objects. For adjective endings, the dative has a beautifully simple rule: no matter what gender or article, the adjective always ends in -en in the dative. This is the easiest ending to memorise at A2 level. Prepositions like in, an, auf, über, unter, neben, zwischen, vor, hinter take dative when expressing location (where?) and accusative when expressing movement toward (where to?). For A2, the location use (dative) with adjective -en is the most tested.

exercises.question: Fatima lives in a small apartment. How do you say "in a small apartment"? (Wohnung = feminine)
exercises.hint: In + dative. Wohnung is feminine. Indefinite article in dative feminine = einer.
exercises.explanation: "in einer kleinen Wohnung" — in takes the dative case (location). Wohnung is feminine. Dative feminine indefinite article = einer. After einer (dative) → adjective ALWAYS ends in -en. This is the golden dative rule: all adjectives in dative get -en.
exercises.rule_hint: Dative: adjective ending is ALWAYS -en, regardless of gender. in einer kleinen Wohnung, mit einem alten Bus, bei dem netten Freund.

lessons.title: The 10 key Goethe A2 adjectives
lessons.content: These adjectives appear in nearly every Goethe A2 exam. Learn them in their three main forms — nominative masculine after ein (the "hero" form showing -er), nominative feminine after eine, and dative after einem/einer (-en for all). Once you know these, you cover the majority of adjective questions in A2 Lesen and Schreiben.

exercises.question: Amina bought a new dress. How do you say "a new dress"? (Kleid = neuter)
exercises.hint: Kleid is neuter. "a" = ein. The verb kaufen takes accusative. Accusative neuter = same as nominative neuter.
exercises.explanation: "ein neues Kleid" — Kleid is neuter. Accusative neuter after ein has the same form as nominative neuter: -es. The -es ending signals "this noun is neuter" — because ein alone could be masculine or neuter. So the adjective must carry the gender signal: -es.
exercises.rule_hint: After ein + neuter (nominative or accusative): adjective ending = -es. ein neues Kleid, ein altes Haus, ein gutes Restaurant.

lessons.title: Kwame, Amina, Kofi, Fatima — all patterns, real mistakes
lessons.content: The most common error for African English speakers is using the adjective with no ending at all — treating it like an English adjective. "ein gut Mann" sounds natural to an English speaker but is wrong in German. The second most common error is using the wrong ending (-e instead of -er for ein + masc. nom.).

exercises.question: Which sentence is completely correct? Kofi wants to describe his interesting new city.
exercises.hint: Think about the gender of Stadt (feminine) and case (nominative after ist = subject complement).
exercises.explanation: "Berlin ist eine interessante Stadt." — Stadt is feminine. After eine + feminine nominative → adjective ending is -e. The verb "ist" with a predicate noun uses nominative. So: eine (fem. nom. article) + interessant + -e = interessante. Not -er (that is for masculine after ein), not -es (that is for neuter after ein).
exercises.rule_hint: After eine + feminine nominative: adjective ending = -e. eine interessante Stadt, eine alte Frau, eine schöne Wohnung.

title: SubordinateClauses
description: A subordinate clause connects two ideas into one sentence. The four conjunctions that create them — weil, dass, wenn, ob — each trigger the same golden rule: the verb moves to the very end of the clause. This single rule is the most-tested sentence structure on the Goethe A2 exam.

lessons.title: The golden rule: verb goes to the end
lessons.content: A subordinate clause is a second sentence that you attach to a main sentence using a conjunction. In English, the word order stays the same in both halves. In German, one rule changes everything: when a conjunction starts a subordinate clause, the verb in that clause moves to the very last position.

exercises.question: What happens to the verb in a subordinate clause after weil, dass, wenn, or ob?
exercises.hint: Think about how German word order in a subordinate clause differs from a main clause.
options: It stays in position 2, just like in a main clause
It disappears — you only need one verb per sentence
It moves to the very END of the subordinate clause
It moves to position 1, before the subject
exercises.correct_answer: It moves to the very END of the subordinate clause
exercises.explanation: The verb moves to the very END of the subordinate clause. This is the single most important rule for subordinate clauses in German. Compare: "Ich lerne Deutsch." (verb lerne in position 2) vs "...weil ich Deutsch lerne." (verb lerne at the end). Every subordinating conjunction — weil, dass, wenn, ob — triggers this same movement.
exercises.rule_hint: Subordinate clause golden rule: conjunction + subject + [middle] + VERB (end). The verb ALWAYS goes to the last position after weil/dass/wenn/ob.

lessons.title: weil — because
lessons.content: weil introduces the reason for the action or state in the main clause. After weil, the entire verb — including modals, separable prefixes, and Perfekt auxiliaries — moves to the very end of the clause.

exercises.question: Amina learns German because she wants to work in Germany. Which weil sentence is correct?
exercises.hint: After weil, the verb goes to the end. With a modal (möchte), the modal goes LAST and the infinitive goes just before it.
options: Amina lernt Deutsch, weil sie möchte in Deutschland arbeiten.
Amina lernt Deutsch, weil sie in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
Amina lernt Deutsch weil sie in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
Amina lernt Deutsch, weil in Deutschland arbeiten sie möchte.
exercises.correct_answer: Amina lernt Deutsch, weil sie in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
exercises.explanation: "Amina lernt Deutsch, weil sie in Deutschland arbeiten möchte." — Two rules applied: (1) comma before weil, (2) modal möchte goes to the very end, infinitive arbeiten goes just before it. Option A keeps möchte in position 2 — the most common error. Option C is missing the comma. Option D has wrong word order in the subordinate clause.
exercises.rule_hint: weil + modal: weil + subject + [middle] + INFINITIVE + MODAL(last). ...weil sie in Deutschland arbeiten möchte. Modal always last.

lessons.title: dass — that
lessons.content: dass introduces a subordinate clause that reports what someone thinks, says, knows, or believes. In English you can often drop "that" — "I think (that) German is difficult." In German, dass cannot be omitted, and the verb always moves to the end.

exercises.question: Kofi says he is coming tomorrow. Which dass sentence is correct?
exercises.hint: dass sends the verb to the end. Remember the comma. Check: where does "kommt" go?
options: Kofi sagt, dass er kommt morgen.
Kofi sagt dass er morgen kommt.
Kofi sagt, dass er morgen kommt.
Kofi sagt, dass morgen er kommt.
exercises.correct_answer: Kofi sagt, dass er morgen kommt.
exercises.explanation: "Kofi sagt, dass er morgen kommt." — After dass, the verb kommt goes to the very end of the clause. The comma before dass is required. Option A puts kommt before morgen — wrong, the verb must be last. Option B is missing the comma before dass. Option D puts the adverb morgen before the subject — unusual and unnatural.
exercises.rule_hint: dass + verb to end: Kofi sagt, dass er morgen kommt. Adverbs and objects stay in the middle; verb goes last.

lessons.title: wenn — when / if
lessons.content: wenn has two uses: it can express a condition (if something is true…) or a repeated time (whenever/each time something happens…). Both use the same word order: verb goes to the end of the wenn-clause. There is one additional rule unique to wenn: when the wenn-clause comes first in the sentence (before the main clause), the main clause verb moves to position 1 — directly after the comma. This is called inversion.

exercises.question: The wenn-clause comes first: "Wenn Kwame Zeit hat, ___." He learns German. Which is correct?
exercises.hint: When the wenn-clause comes first, the main clause undergoes inversion: verb comes BEFORE the subject.
exercises.explanation: "Wenn Kwame Zeit hat, lernt er Deutsch." — When the wenn-clause takes position 1 in the sentence, the main clause verb must come directly after the comma (position 2), BEFORE the subject. So: lernt (verb) + er (subject) + Deutsch. Not "er lernt" — that is not inverted. This inversion rule applies whenever any subordinate clause comes first.
exercises.rule_hint: Wenn-clause first → inversion in main clause: verb comes first, then subject. Wenn X ...-VERB, MAIN-VERB + subject + ...

lessons.title: ob — whether / if
lessons.content: ob turns a direct yes/no question into an indirect question embedded inside a sentence. In English: "Is he coming?" → "I don't know whether he is coming." In German: "Kommt er?" → "Ich weiß nicht, ob er kommt." The same verb-to-end rule applies.

exercises.question: Fatima asks whether the bus is on time. Which sentence is correct?
exercises.hint: ob introduces an indirect yes/no question. The verb goes to the end of the ob-clause.
options: Fatima fragt, ob ist der Bus pünktlich.
Fatima fragt, ob der Bus pünktlich ist.
Fatima fragt ob der Bus pünktlich ist.
Fatima fragt, ob der Bus ist pünktlich.
exercises.correct_answer: Fatima fragt, ob der Bus pünktlich ist.
exercises.explanation: "Fatima fragt, ob der Bus pünktlich ist." — ob sends the verb ist to the end of the clause. Comma before ob is required. Option A keeps ist in position 2 (as if it were a direct question) — wrong. Option C is missing the comma. Option D places ist before pünktlich — wrong, the verb must be at the very end.
exercises.rule_hint: ob clause: ob + subject + [middle/adjective] + VERB(end). Fatima fragt, ob der Bus pünktlich ist. Comma before ob always.

lessons.title: Kwame, Amina, Kofi, Fatima — all four conjunctions
lessons.content: The #1 mistake for African English speakers writing A2 Schreiben: keeping the verb in position 2 after weil. "Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich möchte in Deutschland arbeiten." This sounds natural in English but is penalised in every German exam. The verb must go to the END.

exercises.question: Which sentence is completely correct — right comma, right word order, right conjunction?
exercises.hint: Check: comma present? Verb at end of subordinate clause? Correct conjunction (weil not wenn for a reason)?
options: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich möchte in Deutschland arbeiten.
Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
Ich lerne Deutsch weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
Ich lerne Deutsch, wenn ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
exercises.correct_answer: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
exercises.explanation: "Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte." — Correct on all counts: (1) comma before weil, (2) weil is the right conjunction for giving a reason, (3) modal möchte is at the very end of the subordinate clause with infinitive arbeiten just before it. Option A keeps möchte in position 2 — the #1 weil mistake. Option C is missing the comma. Option D uses wenn (when/if) instead of weil (because) — wrong conjunction for a reason.
exercises.rule_hint: Perfect weil sentence: MAIN CLAUSE, weil SUBJECT [middle] INFINITIVE MODAL. Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.

title: Comparative &Superlative
description: To say "bigger", "faster", "the best" in German you don't use a separate word like English "more" or "most". Instead, you add an ending directly to the adjective: schnell → schneller. For the superlative: schnell → am schnellsten. There are also irregular forms — gut, viel, gern — that must be memorised. Both comparative and superlative appear in A2 Schreiben tasks whenever you describe preferences or make comparisons.

lessons.title: Forming the comparative
lessons.content: The comparative in German is simple: add -er to the adjective. That's it. Unlike English, you never use a separate word like "more". "More expensive" is not "mehr teuer" — it is teurer. Many one-syllable adjectives with the vowels a, o, or u add an umlaut in the comparative. This must be memorised — it cannot be predicted by a rule.

exercises.question: How do you say "faster" in German? (schnell = fast)
exercises.hint: German never uses "mehr" to form comparatives. It adds a suffix directly to the adjective.
exercises.explanation: "schneller" — German comparative: add -er directly to the adjective. schnell + er = schneller. "mehr schnell" is the literal English translation trap — this is wrong in German. "am schnellsten" is the superlative (the fastest), not the comparative. "schnellst" is an incomplete superlative stem.
exercises.rule_hint: Comparative formula: adjective + -er. Never use "mehr" before an adjective. schnell → schneller, teuer → teurer, interessant → interessanter.

lessons.title: als vs so…wie
lessons.content: Once you have the comparative form, you need the right word to connect the two things you are comparing. German uses two different constructions for "than" and "as…as" — and they are never interchangeable.

exercises.question: Berlin ist ___ als Nairobi. (groß — big)
exercises.hint: groß is irregular in the comparative — it adds umlaut. Comparative of groß = ?
options: Berlin ist mehr groß als Nairobi.
Berlin ist am größten als Nairobi.
exercises.explanation: "Berlin ist größer als Nairobi." — groß adds an umlaut in the comparative: groß → größer. Use als after the comparative form to mean "than". Option B uses mehr groß — never correct. Option C uses am größten which is the superlative, not comparative. Option D has "großer" without umlaut — incorrect.
exercises.rule_hint: groß → größer → am größten. Umlaut required. Comparative + als for unequal comparisons.

lessons.title: Forming the superlative
lessons.content: The superlative means "the most" or "the -est". German has two superlative forms depending on how you use the adjective in the sentence. When the superlative comes before a noun, it behaves like any adjective — it takes the regular adjective endings from Module 15. The base is the superlative stem (schnellst-, ältst-).

exercises.question: Which sentence correctly says "Kofi is as tall as Kwame"? (groß = tall/big)
exercises.hint: Equal comparison uses so…wie with the BASE form of the adjective — no -er ending.
options: Kofi ist so groß wie Kwame.
Kofi ist als groß wie Kwame.
Kofi ist so großer wie Kwame.
exercises.correct_answer: Kofi ist so groß wie Kwame.
exercises.explanation: "Kofi ist so groß wie Kwame." — Equal comparison uses so + BASE FORM + wie. No -er ending on groß because they are equal. Option A uses größer als which means "taller than" — not equal. Option C puts als before the adjective — wrong. Option D uses großer (comparative form) with so…wie — you never use the comparative form with so…wie.
exercises.rule_hint: Equal comparison: so + adjective (BASE FORM, no -er) + wie. Kofi ist so groß wie Kwame. Unequal: adjective-er + als.

lessons.title: Irregular forms — must memorise
lessons.content: Six adjectives/adverbs have completely irregular comparative and superlative forms — just like English "good → better → best". These cannot be predicted from the base form. They appear in almost every A2 exam and must be learned by heart.

exercises.question: What is the correct superlative of "alt" (old) in the predicative form?
exercises.hint: alt → älter. For the superlative, the stem ends in -t so you need -esten (not -sten) for pronunciation.
exercises.explanation: "am ältesten" — alt adds umlaut in comparative and superlative: alt → älter → am ältesten. Because the stem ends in -t, you add -esten (not -sten) for pronunciation. "am altest" has no umlaut. "am altsten" has no umlaut and wrong suffix. "am ältsten" has umlaut but is missing the -e- buffer.
exercises.rule_hint: Superlative with -esten: stems ending in -d/-t/-s/-ß/-z/-sch add -esten. alt → am ältesten, heiß → am heißesten.

lessons.title: Comparative before a noun
lessons.content: When the comparative or superlative comes directly before a noun, it behaves exactly like any attributive adjective — it takes a declension ending from Module 15. The comparative stem is adjective + -er, and you add the case ending after that. The most tested comparative structure in A2 Schreiben is expressing preferences using gern → lieber → am liebsten. Learn this three-part pattern and use it whenever a task asks "Was magst du?" or "Was ist dein Lieblings…?"

exercises.question: Fatima likes fufu best of all. Which sentence uses the correct form of gern?
exercises.hint: gern is irregular: gern → lieber → am liebsten. "Best of all / most of all" = ?
exercises.explanation: "Fatima mag Fufu am liebsten." — gern has completely irregular forms: gern (like) → lieber (prefer) → am liebsten (like best of all). None of these can be predicted from the base form. "am gernsten" would be a regular superlative of gern — but gern is irregular. "mehr gern" applies the English pattern — wrong. "am liebern" is not a real word.
exercises.rule_hint: Irregular: gern → lieber → am liebsten. Fatima mag Fufu am liebsten. Use for expressing what you like best.

lessons.title: Kwame, Amina, Kofi, Fatima — comparing everything
lessons.content: Cities, food, transport, language, weather — the comparative and superlative appear whenever you describe preferences or make comparisons. These are the most common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.question: Which of these sentences is completely correct?
exercises.hint: Check: right comparative form? right word (als or wie after comparative)? right irregular form?
options: Lagos ist mehr groß als Accra.
Lagos ist so größer wie Accra.
exercises.explanation: "Lagos ist größer als Accra." — Two rules correct: (1) groß → größer (umlaut comparative, no "mehr"), (2) als after the comparative form for "than". Option A uses "mehr groß" — never correct, always adjective + -er. Option B uses "wie" after a comparative — always use "als" after comparative. Option D mixes so…wie (equal comparison) with größer (comparative) — the two patterns must not be mixed.
exercises.rule_hint: Unequal comparison: adjective + -er + als. Lagos ist größer als Accra. Never "mehr groß", never "größer wie".

title: ReflexiveVerbs
description: Reflexive verbs describe actions you do to yourself. "I wash myself", "I feel myself", "I introduce myself". In German, these verbs always travel with a reflexive pronoun — mich or mir for ich, dich or dir for du, and sich for all third-person forms. Forgetting this pronoun is the single biggest African learner mistake with these verbs.

lessons.title: What are reflexive verbs?
lessons.content: A reflexive verb describes an action where the subject and the object are the same person — you do the action to yourself. In English you sometimes say "I wash myself" — the "myself" makes it reflexive. In German, this reflexive meaning is built into the verb: the verb waschen (to wash) becomes sich waschen (to wash oneself). The reflexive pronoun comes immediately after the conjugated verb — just like any other object pronoun.

exercises.question: Which sentence uses the reflexive verb correctly?
exercises.hint: A reflexive verb needs its reflexive pronoun. Check which sentence has the pronoun in the right place.
exercises.explanation: "Er wäscht sich täglich." — The reflexive pronoun comes immediately after the conjugated verb. Option A puts sich before the verb — wrong word order. Option C puts sich at the end — wrong. Option D inverts the subject and pronoun — not possible. The verb is always position 2 in a main clause, and the reflexive pronoun follows it directly.
exercises.rule_hint: Reflexive pronoun position: subject + conjugated verb + reflexive pronoun. Er wäscht sich. Ich wasche mich.

lessons.title: All reflexive pronouns
lessons.content: German reflexive pronouns come in two cases — accusative (most common, for the whole action) and dative (used when there is a separate accusative object, like a body part). Most of the time, you use the accusative form.

exercises.question: Which reflexive pronoun goes with "du"?
exercises.hint: du is the familiar singular "you". Remember the two forms that are unique: ich → mich, and du → ?
exercises.explanation: "dich" — du uses dich (accusative) and dir (dative) as its reflexive pronouns. sich is for er/sie/es and sie/Sie. mich is for ich. euch is for ihr (plural you). The forms for ich and du are the only ones that are unique — all other persons share sich, uns, or euch.
exercises.rule_hint: Reflexive pronouns: ich → mich/mir, du → dich/dir, er/sie/es → sich, wir → uns, ihr → euch, sie/Sie → sich.

lessons.title: 8 essential A2 reflexive verbs
lessons.content: These eight reflexive verbs appear frequently in Goethe A2 exam tasks — in Lesen texts, Hören recordings, and Schreiben prompts. Master all eight and you will recognise them immediately. Notice that sich anziehen is also a separable verb — an- separates to the end of the clause.

exercises.question: What does "sich freuen" mean?
exercises.hint: Context: Amina bekommt gute Noten. Amina freut sich. Think about her emotion.
options: to be happy / look forward to
exercises.correct_answer: to be happy / look forward to
exercises.explanation: "to be happy / look forward to" — sich freuen has two preposition variants: sich freuen über = to be happy about something (past/present event), and sich freuen auf = to look forward to something (future event). In both cases it expresses positive emotion. sich waschen = to wash oneself. sich setzen = to sit down. sich vorstellen = to introduce oneself.
exercises.rule_hint: sich freuen über + Akk = to be happy about. sich freuen auf + Akk = to look forward to. Both always need the reflexive pronoun.

lessons.title: Accusative vs dative reflexive
lessons.content: Most reflexive verbs use the accusative form — the pronoun refers to the whole action being done to yourself. However, when the sentence already has another accusative object (usually a specific body part), the reflexive pronoun switches to the dative.

exercises.question: Ich wasche ___ die Hände. Which reflexive pronoun correctly fills the blank?
